Tariff designElectricity System Services Market
Library

Flexibility connection agreement · review dimension

⚙

Operational interface

How will limits be forecast, communicated, controlled, verified, and made fail-safe?

Understand the dimensionComment on this dimension

Clear definition

What this dimension means

The operational interface converts the contract into real-time or scheduled action. It includes metering, telemetry, communication, forecasting, set-points, local controllers, cybersecurity, fallback behaviour, and verification.

A flexible access right is credible only if both parties can implement it safely. Poor communication or unclear control priority can create network risk, asset damage, or disputes.

Design choices

Choices that must be made explicitly

A reviewer should be able to identify which option is selected, why it is selected, who decides it, and how it is implemented.

Control mode

Static export limit, scheduled profile, remote set-point, dynamic operating envelope, or trip signal.

Communication

Portal, API, SCADA, smart meter, direct controller, or aggregator interface.

Update horizon

Long-term schedule, day-ahead, intraday, periodic real-time update, or emergency action.

Fail-safe

Default capacity, local autonomous limit, safe disconnection, or temporary firm fallback.

Practical example

How the dimension appears in practice

A battery receives a dynamic import/export envelope every 15 minutes. The agreement must define latency, acknowledgement, missing-message behaviour, local safety limits, and the evidence used for settlement or dispute.

Review principle

The mechanism is credible only when the selected design can be explained in plain language and linked to a verifiable network, regulatory, commercial, or customer outcome.

Stakeholder implications

The same design choice creates different responsibilities and risks

DSO

Maintain secure, reliable, and scalable control.

Regulator

Set interoperability and proportionality expectations.

Developer or aggregator

Integrate DSO limits with plant and portfolio optimization.

Customer or FSP

Know equipment, maintenance, cybersecurity, and liability obligations.

Questions for reviewers

What remains open?

  • What happens when communication fails?
  • Which controller has priority?
  • How are set-points acknowledged and verified?
  • Who pays for equipment and upgrades?
  • Which cybersecurity standard is appropriate?

Evidence expected

What should support the decision?

  • Latency and reliability tests
  • Fail-safe and recovery testing
  • Interoperability demonstration
  • Cybersecurity risk assessment
  • Operational responsibility matrix

Feedback on operational interface

What should be clarified, added, removed, or redesigned?

Please comment specifically on the definition, design choices, stakeholder responsibilities, implementation feasibility, evidence requirements, and interaction with the other flexibility acquisition mechanisms.

Submit structured feedback
